repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem

`:intro` is not distinctive and does not give an impression of the Neovim character/brand.

## Solution

~~Add emojis.~~ Use echa's gift.

Disclosure: This PR was developed by a human who produces his own electricity by converting oats into blood.

comment_data.body: Why no translation for this line? At least "type" and "news" should be translated.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -4197,8 +4197,7 @@ void intro_message(bool colon)
     N_("type  :checkhealth<Enter>     to optimize Nvim"),
     N_("type  :q<Enter>               to exit         "),
     N_("type  :help<Enter>            for help        "),
-    "",
-    N_("type  :help news<Enter> to see changes in v%s.%s"),
+    "type  :help news<Enter>       ✨ v%s.%s news ✨",

comment_data.body: I think this has one extra space indent? (At least that's how it renders for me: that line is shifted to the right by one column.)
comment_data.diff_hunk:

     N_("type  :checkhealth<Enter>     to optimize Nvim"),

comment_regular_data.body: previous discussion: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38327#discussion_r2943816317

I've wanted a more distinctive intro screen for a long time. Since there is an interest in this, I've made some experiments **in Lua** as I have no clue how to do it in C. Here are couple of suggestions from the least to the most snazzy:
Basic (current mockup)
Colored
Colored and bordered
Colored and logo
Colored and logo and bordered
Here is a Lua code for further experiments:
Lua code for snazzy intro ```lua --stylua: ignore local basic_lines = { ' NVIM v0.12.0', -- 1 '', -- 2 ' Nvim is open source and freely distributable ', -- 3 ' https://neovim.io/#chat', -- 4 '', -- 5 'type :help nvim if you are new! ', -- 6 'type :checkhealth to optimize Nvim ', -- 7 'type :q to exit ', -- 8 'type :help for help ', -- 9 '', -- 10 'type :help news v0.12 news ', -- 11 '', -- 12 ' Help poor children in Uganda!', -- 13 'type :help Kuwasha for information ', -- 14 -- 01234567890123456789012345678901234567890123456789 } -- Items are { row, col_from, col_to, hl_group } -- Row-col 1-0 indexed, end exclusive local basic_hl_ranges = { -- keys { 6, 16, 23, 'SpecialKey' }, { 7, 18, 25, 'SpecialKey' }, { 8, 8, 15, 'SpecialKey' }, { 9, 11, 18, 'SpecialKey' }, { 11, 16, 23, 'SpecialKey' }, { 14, 19, 26, 'SpecialKey' }, } local colored_hl_ranges = vim.deepcopy(basic_hl_ranges) colored_hl_ranges = vim.list_extend(colored_hl_ranges, { -- Version { 1, 16, 28, 'String' }, -- Link to chat { 4, 12, 35, 'Underlined' }, -- `:` { 6, 6, 7, 'SpecialKey' }, { 7, 6, 7, 'SpecialKey' }, { 8, 6, 7, 'SpecialKey' }, { 9, 6, 7, 'SpecialKey' }, { 11, 6, 7, 'SpecialKey' }, { 14, 6, 7, 'SpecialKey' }, -- Typed command { 6, 7, 16, 'Identifier' }, { 7, 7, 18, 'Identifier' }, { 8, 7, 8, 'Identifier' }, { 9, 7, 11, 'Identifier' }, { 11, 7, 16, 'Identifier' }, { 14, 7, 19, 'Identifier' }, }) local ns_id = vim.api.nvim_create_namespace('intro') local show_centered_flloat = function(lines, hl_ranges) -- Show only single centered float for _, win_id in ipairs(vim.api.nvim_list_wins()) do if vim.api.nvim_win_get_config(win_id).relative ~= '' then vim.api.nvim_win_close(win_id, true) end end -- Compute config data local width = 0 for _, l in ipairs(lines) do width = math.max(width, vim.fn.strdisplaywidth(l)) end local border_offset = vim.o.winborder == '' and 0 or 2 local row = math.floor(0.5 * (vim.o.lines - #lines - border_offset)) local col = math.floor(0.5 * (vim.o.columns - width - border_offset)) -- Show local buf_id = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, true) vim.api.nvim_buf_set_lines(buf_id, 0, -1, false, lines) vim.bo[buf_id].bufhidden = 'wipe' local config = { relative = 'editor', row = row, col = col, height = #lines, width = width, style = 'minimal' } local win_id = vim.api.nvim_open_win(buf_id, false, config) -- Highlight vim.wo[win_id].winhighlight = 'NormalFloat:Normal' for _, range in ipairs(hl_ranges or {}) do local extmark_opts = { end_row = range[1] - 1, end_col = range[3], hl_group = range[4] } vim.api.nvim_buf_set_extmark(buf_id, ns_id, range[1] - 1, range[2], extmark_opts) end return win_id end _G.basic = function() show_centered_flloat(basic_lines, basic_hl_ranges) end _G.colored = function() show_centered_flloat(basic_lines, colored_hl_ranges) end _G.colored_bordered = function() vim.o.winborder = 'rounded' _G.colored() end _G.colored_logo = function() local lines = vim.list_extend({ ' │ ╲ ││', ' ││╲╲││', ' ││ ╲ │', '', }, basic_lines) local hl_ranges = vim.tbl_map(function(r) return { r[1] + 4, r[2], r[3], r[4] } end, colored_hl_ranges) hl_ranges = vim.list_extend(hl_ranges, { -- Box drawing characters have byte length 3 { 1, 19, 22, 'Special' }, { 1, 23, 26, 'String' }, { 1, 27, 33, 'String' }, { 2, 19, 25, 'Special' }, { 2, 25, 37, 'String' }, { 3, 19, 25, 'Special' }, { 3, 26, 29, 'String' }, { 3, 30, 33, 'String' }, }) show_centered_flloat(lines, hl_ranges) end _G.colored_logo_bordered = function() vim.o.winborder = 'rounded' _G.colored_logo() end ```
Notes: - Maybe not rounded but regular `'single'` border would be more robust with respect to terminals and fonts. I've seen rounded corners have rendering problems. - Highlight groups used: `String` for green, `Special` for cyan, `Underlined` for link, `Identifier` for blue, `SpecialKey` for cyan in special key (`:` and ``). All of them are very basic and should be available in all color schemes. This also means that logo will not be strictly cyan-green, but I think it is even better. --- I personally like the most snazzy one here (colored + logo + bordered).
I LOVE the logo mockup @echasnovski , holy shit we are doing that.
Border is unnecessarily distracting.

There is also a possible middle ground between "no borders" and "yes borders" with "yes dotted separators" (which is also a box drawing character so should have a fairly good support):
Colored and logo and separators
Separator lines can also be highlighted. Like with `WinSeparator` (no effect in default color scheme, but might be in others) or `NonText` (to hide them a bit). --- I personally still like bordered one more, but that is ultimately a matter of taste. Not a strong opinion.

The border makes it look like a floating window to me.
It might be confusing.
in terms of layout, WDYT about this:

Placing the text on the right makes the content feel more formal; at a glance, the logo appears decorative because the visual focus is on the text. In contrast, centering the logo serves only to show off the brand. It likely depends on the effect we want to convey, but I personally lean toward centering the logo.

> in terms of layout, WDYT about this:

I *definitely* like that there is less vertical space (12 vs 16-17 if centered vs 14 on `master`). However, I'd personally give more weight on how it looks on release version. I.e. with `NVIM v0.12.0` to its right. If top three lines are centered separately, then I'd prefer this one indeed, but if justified to the left - probably not.

About `v0.12 changelog` change. Maybe it is a good idea to preserve the `type ... for/to ...` template? Like `type  :help news<Enter>  for v0.12 changes`? Makes it on cell wider than the widest other line, but it reads better.

And there can be less whitespace between `<Enter>` and what is to its right.

> in terms of layout, WDYT about this

Much less sexy than the centered version. (IMO "colored and logo" is as perfect as it gets.)

Also, please don't remove the "Nvim is open source and freely distributable" line.
@echasnovski's logo proposal looks awesome! Also opens the door for showing the logo using `vim.ui.img` on supported terminals

https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38345#issuecomment-4081374381 Colored + Logo is my favourite by far. It is incredibly stylish. 

The more condensed variant by justinmk is also nice, but I'll second that keeping the "Neovim is open source and freely distributable" line is a must

> keeping the "Neovim is open source and freely distributable" line is a must

I find it funny that people care about this. That line does not decide anything about the project. Obvious Neovim is open source and will stay that way.

> That line does not decide anything about the project. Obvious Neovim is open source and will stay that way.

Obviously obvious. It's a statement of values though.

> > That line does not decide anything about the project. Obvious Neovim is open source and will stay that way.
> 
> Obviously obvious. It's a statement of values though.

In the same sense that we keep the "help children in Uganda" stuff after both the Bram's passing and the ICCF thing closing doors / moving places. It's just part of the spirit of (Neo)vim. I'm not panicked about a sudden malevolent license switch or anything.

I don't forget about #9933 and I am concerned about the "corporate friendly" steps, compared to which the above-mentioned sentence about freedom remains only a facade (although is still important). I don't feel prepared to discuss the possibility of publishing forks like "Nvim premium", but it seems that the Apache license allows it.

> I am concerned about the "corporate friendly" steps, compared to which the above-mentioned sentence about freedom remains only a facade (although is still important). I don't feel prepared to discuss the possibility of publishing forks like "Nvim premium", but it seems that the Apache license allows it.

That's a wild, nonsense extrapolation that almost intentionally misinterprets the words that I wrote.

We will keep the line. That wasn't my point.

> > I am concerned about the "corporate friendly" steps, compared to which the above-mentioned sentence about freedom remains only a facade (although is still important). I don't feel prepared to discuss the possibility of publishing forks like "Nvim premium", but it seems that the Apache license allows it.
> 
> That's a wild, nonsense extrapolation that almost intentionally misinterprets the words that I wrote.
> 
> We will keep the line. That wasn't my point.

To be clear: this is not about your intention here - this clearly symbolic (perhaps aesthetic) proposal merely prompted me to express my (I don't know how justified) concerns about licensing guidelines.
Going to try the left-aligned thing for now. If someone wants to rework it in the next few days feel free.

> Going to try the left-aligned thing for now. If someone wants to rework it in the next few days feel free. Just a suggestion for a variant to test: The second "header" line is quite long so this can look a bit unbalanced; maybe you could try a "hanging logo" (i.e., all lines left-aligned, with the logo sitting to the left of the text block)?
continued at https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38378
